hits.snippet: April 26, 2020 - Appreciate the conversation with American Medical Association and Marc Zarefsky. A lot of my work at Geisinger right now is centered around something simple, but not always easy: reducing friction. It shows up at every level of a healthcare organization: system, service line, clinic and even down to the unit. And most of the time, it’s not anything major. It’s small things that add up, like workflows that no longer connect, processes that slow people down or gaps between teams. As a chief medical officer, my job is to find those points and eliminate them so physicians and care teams can focus on what matters most: delivering exceptional care.

hits.title: Steve Silvestro, MD, DABOM, FAAP - Ro | LinkedIn
hits.snippet: October 16, 2018 - The CPOM lie health tech startup founders WANT to believe: “If the money is in the PC account, we can just pay it out as MSO fees.” Here’s what I’ve learned (the hard way) about “management fees” under Corporate Practice of Medicine (CPOM) / fee-splitting frameworks: A management fee is supposed to be payment for non-clinical services the MSO provides—like billing support, scheduling, admin staff, HR, compliance ops, marketing (where allowed), vendor management, office services, IT.... That sounds simple… until money starts moving. It does not necessarily mean the MSO can’t use funds sitting in the PC’s bank account. In many real-world structures, the MSO is the operational engine and may pay legitimate PC expenses (payroll, rent, software, vendors, etc.) from the PC account as the PC’s agent—if the agreements, approvals, and controls support it.

hits.title: Cheng Ruan, MD - Synaptik Health | LinkedIn
hits.snippet: 5 days ago - The CPOM lie health tech startup founders WANT to believe: “If the money is in the PC account, we can just pay it out as MSO fees.” Here’s what I’ve learned (the hard way) about “management fees” under Corporate Practice of Medicine (CPOM) / fee-splitting frameworks: A management fee is supposed to be payment for non-clinical services the MSO provides—like billing support, scheduling, admin staff, HR, compliance ops, marketing (where allowed), vendor management, office services, IT.... That sounds simple… until money starts moving. It does not necessarily mean the MSO can’t use funds sitting in the PC’s bank account. In many real-world structures, the MSO is the operational engine and may pay legitimate PC expenses (payroll, rent, software, vendors, etc.) from the PC account as the PC’s agent—if the agreements, approvals, and controls support it.

hits.title: Alexander Schloe MD - Alpenglow Family Medicine Direct Primary Care | LinkedIn
hits.snippet: October 10, 2025 - The CPOM lie health tech startup founders WANT to believe: “If the money is in the PC account, we can just pay it out as MSO fees.” Here’s what I’ve learned (the hard way) about “management fees” under Corporate Practice of Medicine (CPOM) / fee-splitting frameworks: A management fee is supposed to be payment for non-clinical services the MSO provides—like billing support, scheduling, admin staff, HR, compliance ops, marketing (where allowed), vendor management, office services, IT.... That sounds simple… until money starts moving. It does not necessarily mean the MSO can’t use funds sitting in the PC’s bank account. In many real-world structures, the MSO is the operational engine and may pay legitimate PC expenses (payroll, rent, software, vendors, etc.) from the PC account as the PC’s agent—if the agreements, approvals, and controls support it.

hits.title: Steven Williams - American Society of Plastic Surgeons (ASPS) | LinkedIn
hits.snippet: March 30, 2026 - The CPOM lie health tech startup founders WANT to believe: “If the money is in the PC account, we can just pay it out as MSO fees.” Here’s what I’ve learned (the hard way) about “management fees” under Corporate Practice of Medicine (CPOM) / fee-splitting frameworks: A management fee is supposed to be payment for non-clinical services the MSO provides—like billing support, scheduling, admin staff, HR, compliance ops, marketing (where allowed), vendor management, office services, IT.... That sounds simple… until money starts moving. It does not necessarily mean the MSO can’t use funds sitting in the PC’s bank account. In many real-world structures, the MSO is the operational engine and may pay legitimate PC expenses (payroll, rent, software, vendors, etc.) from the PC account as the PC’s agent—if the agreements, approvals, and controls support it.

hits.title: Ashok Roy, MD, MPH, MBA - Survey Health | LinkedIn
hits.snippet: 1 week ago - The CPOM lie health tech startup founders WANT to believe: “If the money is in the PC account, we can just pay it out as MSO fees.” Here’s what I’ve learned (the hard way) about “management fees” under Corporate Practice of Medicine (CPOM) / fee-splitting frameworks: A management fee is supposed to be payment for non-clinical services the MSO provides—like billing support, scheduling, admin staff, HR, compliance ops, marketing (where allowed), vendor management, office services, IT.... That sounds simple… until money starts moving. It does not necessarily mean the MSO can’t use funds sitting in the PC’s bank account. In many real-world structures, the MSO is the operational engine and may pay legitimate PC expenses (payroll, rent, software, vendors, etc.) from the PC account as the PC’s agent—if the agreements, approvals, and controls support it.

hits.title: Katrina Firlik, MD - Writer, neurosurgeon-turned-entrepreneur
hits.snippet: 1 month ago - The CPOM lie health tech startup founders WANT to believe: “If the money is in the PC account, we can just pay it out as MSO fees.” Here’s what I’ve learned (the hard way) about “management fees” under Corporate Practice of Medicine (CPOM) / fee-splitting frameworks: A management fee is supposed to be payment for non-clinical services the MSO provides—like billing support, scheduling, admin staff, HR, compliance ops, marketing (where allowed), vendor management, office services, IT.... That sounds simple… until money starts moving. It does not necessarily mean the MSO can’t use funds sitting in the PC’s bank account. In many real-world structures, the MSO is the operational engine and may pay legitimate PC expenses (payroll, rent, software, vendors, etc.) from the PC account as the PC’s agent—if the agreements, approvals, and controls support it.
